title: Observability First Runtime
use_for: designing runtime systems whose health, readiness, degraded state, and phase behavior are inspectable from the start
making modules, pipelines, integrations, and command boundaries report state consistently
planning diagnostic snapshots and debug endpoints before feature implementation
ensuring failure analysis does not depend on fragile optional features
do_not_use_for: logging-only setup with no runtime health model
performance profiling without readiness or failure visibility concerns
rules: define observable runtime state before feature surfaces depend on it
make readiness, degraded state, and last failure explicit
diagnostics must initialize before fragile integrations
debug endpoints should read snapshots rather than internal mutable state
observability must not become required for domain correctness
procedure: List runtime owners that need visibility: host, modules, integrations, pipelines, command queues, configuration, and UI surfaces.
Define health states, readiness gates, degraded reasons, counters, timings, and last-error fields.
Define diagnostic snapshots that can be read safely by logs, UI, tests, or support tooling.
Define when snapshots update and who owns each diagnostic record.
Ensure diagnostics remain available during partial startup, degraded integrations, and shutdown.
Verify feature logic does not depend on debug UI or logging sinks for correctness.

primary_when: the task is primarily about making a runtime inspectable, diagnosable, or health-aware
must_not_own_when: the task only needs simple log messages
the task is only about visual debug layout

solves: how to design runtime visibility before failures happen
how to expose health and readiness without leaking internals
how to keep diagnostics alive during partial failure

core_principles: A runtime that cannot explain its state is not ready for a rewrite.
Readiness and degraded state are normal runtime states, not exceptional afterthoughts.
Diagnostics should observe runtime ownership, not create hidden ownership.
Debug UI and logs are consumers of observability, not its source of truth.
constraints: Do not make diagnostics depend on optional integrations being healthy.
Do not expose mutable internal collections as debug state.
Do not hide degraded state behind boolean readiness.
Do not bind reusable observability guidance to a named project or local path.
decision_rules: If a subsystem can fail independently, give it a health state and last failure record.
If a feature is gated by external readiness, expose the gate and degraded reason.
If UI needs debug data, publish a diagnostic snapshot.
If a pipeline owns frame work, expose phase timing and last execution status.
If observability adds allocation pressure to hot paths, batch or sample diagnostics deliberately.

avoid_patterns: logs as the only health model

recipes.name: Add observability to a runtime owner
recipes.when: Use when a module, integration, pipeline, or service needs inspectable readiness and failure state.
steps: Name the owner and its lifecycle state.
Define readiness, degraded reason, last failure, and counters or timings.
Make logs and UI consume the snapshot.
Verify failure paths can still update diagnostics.

pitfalls: Adding logs without a state model.
Conflating not initialized, disabled, degraded, and failed states.
Making observability too expensive for repeated runtime paths.
Letting debug surfaces mutate the system they inspect.
review_checklist: Are health, readiness, and degraded reasons explicit?
Can diagnostics survive partial startup failure?
Do debug endpoints expose snapshots rather than internals?
Is observability independent of project-specific names?

references.title: Diagnostics
references.interpretation: Use for early diagnostics, compact trace output, and failure visibility.
references.why: Observability-first runtime design builds on diagnostics but adds state and readiness contracts.

references.title: Runtime Lifecycle
references.interpretation: Use to align health snapshots with runtime phases.
references.why: Runtime state must be observable across startup, steady-state, suspend, and shutdown.
